[[ja:[[Category:Japanese archaeologists]] [[ja:[[Category:Kyoto University faculty]] [[ja:[[Category:People from Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:[[Category:1893 births]] [[ja:[[Category:1983 deaths]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Sueji Umehara]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:[[ja:梅原末治]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Original pages]] to [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)
== References ==
<references/>
== External Links ==
* [http://124.33.215.236/umehara2008/ume_query.html Umehara Archaeological Materials Database (Toyo Bunko)] * [http://124.33.215.236/umeharajpnjomon/umejpjmn_open_srchinput.php Japanese Jomon Period Archaeological Materials (Toyo Bunko)]

He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)
== References ==
<references/>
== External Links ==
* [http://124.33.215.236/umehara2008/ume_query.html Umehara Archaeological Materials Database (Toyo Bunko)] * [http://124.33.215.236/umeharajpnjomon/umejpjmn_open_srchinput.php Japanese Jomon Period Archaeological Materials (Toyo Bunko)]

* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===

revision.text:
'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)
== References ==
<references/>
== External Links ==
* [http://124.33.215.236/umehara2008/ume_query.html Umehara Archaeological Materials Database (Toyo Bunko)] * [http://124.33.215.236/umeharajpnjomon/umejpjmn_open_srchinput.php Japanese Jomon Period Archaeological Materials (Toyo Bunko)]

* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

[[Category:Japanese archaeologists]] [[Category:Kyoto University faculty]] [[Category:People from Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:1893 births]] [[Category:1983 deaths]] [[Category:pp]] [[category:Sueji Umehara]] [[Category:pp]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sueji Umehara]][[category:Sueji Umehara]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Categories to be overwritten]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Sueji Umehara]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:梅原末治]]

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />

His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)

Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)

* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was appointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was appointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was aqqointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was aqqointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was aqqointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was aqqointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==

* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was aqqointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was aqqointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)
== References ==
<references/>
== External Links ==
* [http://124.33.215.236/umehara2008/ume_query.html Umehara Archaeological Materials Database (Toyo Bunko)] * [http://124.33.215.236/umeharajpnjomon/umejpjmn_open_srchinput.php Japanese Jomon Period Archaeological Materials (Toyo Bunko)]

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was aqqointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was aqqointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was aqqointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was aqqointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

[[Category:Japanese archaeologists]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kyoto University faculty]] [[Category:People from Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:1893 births]] [[Category:1983 deaths]] [[Category:qq]] [[category:Sueji Umehara]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sueji Umehara-->[[Category:Sueji Umehara]][[category:Sueji Umehara]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] <!--Sueji Umehara-->[[Category:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:Categorieqq]] [[Category:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:梅原末治]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueji Umehara]][[Category:qq]]

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was a11ointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was a11ointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)
== References ==
<references/>
== External Links ==
* [http://124.33.215.236/umehara2008/ume_query.html Umehara Archaeological Materials Database (Toyo Bunko)] * [http://124.33.215.236/umeharajpnjomon/umejpjmn_open_srchinput.php Japanese Jomon Period Archaeological Materials (Toyo Bunko)]

Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949年以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was a11ointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was a11ointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)

'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949th year 以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was a11ointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was a11ointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Sueji Umehara''' (梅原 末治, August 13, 1893 – February 19, 1983)<ref name=":0">{{Citation|title=本会顧問 梅原末治博士訃|author=有光教一|year=1983-07-01|url=https://doi.org/10.14989/shirin_66_591|publisher=史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)|language=ja|doi=10.14989/shirin_66_591|access-date=2022-08-22}}</ref> was a Japanese {{ill|archaeologist|ja|考古学者}} specializing in East Asian archaeology, particularly in {{ill|Chinese archaeology|ja|中国考古学}}. He served as a professor emeritus at {{ill|Kyoto University|ja|京都大学}}<ref name=":0" /> and was instrumental in establishing the foundations of East Asian archaeology.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=梅原末治 |url=http://www.toyo-bunko.or.jp/library3/shozou/umehara.html |website=www.toyo-bunko.or.jp |access-date=2022-08-22 |publisher=公益財団法人[[東洋文庫]]}}</ref> He earned his Doctor of Letters (文学博士) from Kyoto Imperial University in 1939 for his dissertation, ''A Study of the Bronze Age in China'' (支那青銅器時代の研究).
== Biography ==
Umehara was born in {{ill|Furuichi Village|ja|古市町 (大阪府)}}, {{ill|Minamikawachi District, Osaka|ja|南河内郡}}, Osaka Prefecture (now {{ill|Habikino|ja|羽曳野市}}).<ref name=":1" /> He graduated from Dōshisha Junior High School (now {{ill|Doshisha Junior and Senior High School|ja|同志社中学校・高等学校}}) in 1913.<ref name="rireki">[https://kensaku.kua1.archives.kyoto-u.ac.jp/rireki/?c=detail&id=000149 詳細情報一覧/歴代総長・教授・助教授履歴検索システム－対象：1949th year 以前の在職者―]</ref> In 1914, he became an assistant at the Department of Archaeology at Kyoto Imperial University. In 1921, he was a11ointed as a member of the Archaeological Survey Committee of the {{ill|Government-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}. He joined the {{ill|Kyoto Research Institute|ja|東方文化学院京都研究所}} (now the {{ill|Institute for Research in Humanities, Kyoto University|ja|京都大学人文科学研究所}}) in 1929 and became a lecturer at Kyoto Imperial University.<ref name=":0" /> In June 1933, he was promoted to associate professor in the Department of Archaeology.<ref name="rireki" /> In 1939, he obtained his doctorate and was a11ointed professor.<ref name="rireki" />
After his retirement in 1956, Umehara was named professor emeritus at Kyoto University.<ref name="rireki" />
== Contributions ==
Despite being frail and unable to pursue higher education initially, Umehara studied under {{ill|Kosaku Hamada|ja|濱田耕作}}, the founder of the archaeology department at Kyoto University. Umehara's archaeological research spanned Japan, Korea, and China, where he contributed significantly to the establishment of typological methods in Japanese archaeology.
His distinctive personality led to conflicts with many contemporary archaeologists, including {{ill|Masao Suenaga|ja|末永雅雄}} and {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄}}.
Umehara's son, {{ill|Kaoru Umehara|ja|梅原郁}}, also became a distinguished archaeologist and professor emeritus at Kyoto University, specializing in East Asian history and winning the Japan Academy Prize in 2010.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=梅原郁先生略歴・主要業績目録 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1642/00000645/ |journal=就実大学史学論集 |volume=36 |pages=9-22 |publisher=[[Shujitsu University]]|date=2022-03}}</ref>
== Awards ==
* 1962: {{ill|Asahi Prize|ja|朝日賞}}<ref name=":0" /> * 1963: {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|ja|文化功労者}}<ref name=":0" /> * Order of the Rising Sun, Gold and Silver Star (勲二等旭日重光章)
== Selected Works ==
=== Books ===
* ''Study on Yayoi Pottery Styles'' (1919) * ''Research on Bronze Bells'' (1927) * ''Research on Chinese Ancient Mirrors'' (1931) * ''Collected Research on Chinese Archaeology'' (1938) * ''The Culture of Ancient Korea'' (1946) * ''Archaeological Observations on Ancient East Asia'' (1947) * ''Archaeology over Sixty Years'' (1973)
=== Co-authored and Edited ===
* ''Comprehensive Overview of Ancient Korean Culture'' (1947–1966) * ''The Tombs of Ancient Korea'' (1973)
== References ==
<references/>
== External Links ==
* [http://124.33.215.236/umehara2008/ume_query.html Umehara Archaeological Materials Database (Toyo Bunko)] * [http://124.33.215.236/umeharajpnjomon/umejpjmn_open_srchinput.php Japanese Jomon Period Archaeological Materials (Toyo Bunko)]
<!--Sueji Umehara-->
<!--Sueji Umehara-->
